author: @debunkified
text:
Hi there, I know this video is like ancient, like what? 9 years ago right? Well, perhaps it is immediately obvious that when you pull the metal away then the opposite magnet is still connected to the metal and thereby and effectively I must say reversed or swtiched. 

Is this how Wesley actually portrayed this or am I misunderstanding something here? In his patent pictures the metal appears to be across both magnets and pulled away from both magnets at the exact same time, not just one side. However when thinking logically with what you have shown here by removing only one side, forces appear to be assymetrical, which is actually a good thing!

Any comments? You still around? Have a great one!

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
I sketched up a simple mechanism to make this work [in theory] indefinitely, and with multiple poles being driven at the same time.

Interestingly, I have a decent sized sleeve magnet from an old pump designed for pumping sulfuring acid and the likes which, although still mounted on the motor that drives it, could realistically be used to conduct tests.

I shall have to look through all of my various magnets and what steel I have on hand, and see if I can't make this operate, using the motor armature as a makeshift flywheel.

author: @waynecard2848
text: the term magnetic circuit is also misleading ... as magnetic polarity is purely two dimensional a magnetic field must posses perfect symmetry over the three dimensional mass. Creating a magnetic circuit is simply changing the geometry of the mass reducing the symmetry to a minimum

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate but this doesn't sound right to me - i don't say you are wrong - i have no idea if you are wrong or right - but it doesn't fit with how i see it.

replies.author: @waynecard2848
replies.text: well think of it this way ... when you create a magnetic circuit have you in anyway increased the amount of magnetism present... have you changed the spin of the atoms ... no you have just optimised the symmetry of the magnetic field into a minimal 3 dimensional space.

author: @waynecard2848
text: the scientific explanation of a magnetic field is so poor a representation of the actual facts it is no wonder people come up with these theories. When the small piece of metal is attached to your keeper it has a polarity ... when you approach the keeper to the pole the keepers polarity will change as its polarity is dependent solely on its orientation to the permanent magnets. The small piece of metal will also simultaneously shift its polarity. There is simply no such thing as flux switching.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fair enough - it's all a bit of a guess anyway mate

replies.author: @waynecard2848
replies.text: yes it is a guess but i think it is very important for us to better understand what magnetism is especially for design engineering I cannot stress enough that proper understanding of electromagnetism is critical to good design.

replies.text: ok

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering something I pointed out above is the hysteresis of the material you are holding on there vs the switching time and power of the keeper, ie, the tiny bit of metal is not swapping its semi-permanent magnetic field it picked up, and has to be forced out to reverse it, fast enough to again grab from the same end of the metal bit, (so one end is a north the other is a south in relation to top and bottom hanging ends) and there is not enough magnetic power to overcome gravity dropping it before the other end can swing around and stick back to the keeper, nor grab it back after the magnetic field (in the tiny bit of metal) is overcome by the keeper's magnetic field, now I would say, that all of this means that if you know the hysteresis rate of a given metal, and can time it, you could possibly use this to your advantage when trying to build such a device, but I doubt it would ever amount to much excess power, and the heat generated would probably suck away most of the generated extra current, if there is any at all due to timing.

I have a design I still need to finish rebuilding with the better materials and see how much more power can be gained (before I was using air gaps for timing and switching, and they sucked away most of the excess power of magnet kicking, so it barely spun on its own, to the point even a little LED with a copper coil to try and light it would stop it, and possibly nothing but the coil with no might have even been enough to drag it down to a stop, as I did not try that, though I don't know. just a guess, since the coil has capacitance, I am guessing it would though.  We shall see. I am still working on a method of applying thin layers of difficult to plate metal. :)

author: @justinouellette1366
text: Robert, been watching you for a long time now and I think this must be an old vid and the point I'm raising has likely been raised, put to death, resurrected, come back on the third day and so forth.  I think you're a very well educated chap and applaud your work.   However, falling on my small experience (and the compass needle)  I don't think there is ever a 'neutral' spot... Please forgive me if I'm being dense somehow, but I'd assume the metal piece has an induced charge and as you pull the keeper farther away from the one pole, the strongest force causing its(the metal) induction switches, thus kicking it off since they are now like poled... ?  just looking at the compass needle tells me the same... at no point do we see the needle doing 'what is wants'  it's always being forced into some position relative to the nearest pole.  remember,... the force of attraction is always stronger than the force of repulsion.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: I know I am late but I am guessing that the reason the metal is falling is due to hysteresis (ie the metal bit has a magnetic charge that takes time to be forced out of it by the other field, so for a moment it is as you say the wrong charge, thus it falls off the keeper when the keeper changes polarity, and there is too little charge all around to cause the other end of the bit of metal to flip around and stick again the opposite side, so it just kicks off and falls.

text: magnetism so misunderstood ... the first misconception is magnetic poles ... there are no magnetic poles the is only a dirction or gradient of polarity ... imagine yourself inside a bar magnet if you look towards what convention calls the north pole what would you see (assuming you could see the magnetic polarity ... would you see the north pole of the magnet? no ... you would see the south polarity ... turn around 180deg now facing the south magnet pole and you would see north polarity

replies.author: @PrajnaPranab
replies.text:
Hi Rob. I know this is one of your oldest vids but I hope you still get notifications for it. I think what is happening is that the thin metal is showing a pole from flux leakage while it is in the circuit (or when it is closed to the poles at each end. Then when you have it pulled away at on end you effectively have a bent bar magnet, so the pole where you have pulled it away is now the opposite pole, or effectively the end of the bar magnet. So the plate is polarised according to the polarity of the bar magnet, as you would expect, when it is only connected at one end. As the other end approaches the other pole it begins to form the circuit so that its polarity begins to change as the leakage flux begins to take over from the normal bar magnet flux. I hope you understand what I mean. If not then reply and I'll try to clarify.

Thinking about this vid has inspired another avenue to investigate though. If we oppose two like poles they will repel. If we introduce a sheet of soft iron between them they will both attract towards the iron. If we arrange things so that magnet pairs in opposition are placed around a disc with iron tabs and rotate the disc then as each tab rotates between the magnets they will move towards the disc and as the tab exits the field they will repel again. If we can take energy from the reciprocating magnets we may be able to extract more energy than it takes to rotate the disc.

replies.author: @PrajnaPranab
replies.text: What I have in mind is a rotating disc with soft iron tabs around the periphery, arranged such that the tabs bisect the space between repelling magnets that are fixed to levers that are joined by a rotating coupling to form a scissor joint. The ends of the levers have a pin that engages in slanted slots on the sides of the cylinder forming, effectively,, two opposed Swatch plates. As the levers reciprocate the cylinder is driven to rotate.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: you certainly know your your stuff. It seems they use the oleic acid as a replacement for the ammonia in the ligand copper complex. As far as I read it = the acid replaces the ammonia and acts as an anti oxidant for the copper. It also has some surfactant properties helping the dispersion of the copper and finally it has a phase transition role so you can get the copper from the aqueous matrix and then wash and allow the VOC's to evaporate to make it easier to concentrate the copper.

author: @rogerscottq
text: Please explain your reasoning for use of oleic acid. This seems to be a popular subject within the field of 'nano-particle' research. The aims of which I find specious. And find them 'specious' because of obvious patent-oriented aims, hence profit-oriented objectives. I can conceive all manners of life-defeating issues by human pursuit of 'nano' level invention. We simply don't have a proper scope for comparison with nature in regard to such. Cheap 'knock-offs'. I deem babes playing with TNT.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I agree - in academia if you don't have a good theory you are assumed to be missing something consequently a lot of modern science is theory driven - to my mind a good theory means a paucity of experience - science is all about experiment and observation. Anyhow - i am thinking of making a ferrofluid for another reason and I am awaiting delivery of oleic acid before i continue my ferrofluid experiements - i'll use a bit for this i think.

author: @rogerscottq
text: I very much appreciate your approach to this. In some ways, I'm more a fan of Faraday and Ampere than of later mathematicians interpreting their delineations. I believe much of the latter amounts to a 90 degree diversion from what is actually observable. Thereby, direct experience is forced to evolve complex, compensatory models many degrees away from an easier . . more direct conclusion. That such complexity yields practical results does not, by any means, mean 'best method'. So I'm with you.

author: @rogerscottq
text: It would be interesting to perform the same, somehow, in a ferro-magnetic fluid in order to follow, visually, everything going on. I remember seeing an old wood-cut illustration of an experiment using sulfuric acid in a glass tank with polarized wires in this fluid. Externally, a magnet was brought near one of the wires and the bubbling fluid showed a rotation that seemed to prove Ampere's concept of magnetism. Which, in turn, suggests evidence for the vortex-atom model suggested by Kelvin.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Yes, I thought it was fascinating too. There are three zones - the first is where there is an induced north pole in the keeper - which attracts the metal. Then as you move it nearer the induced pole disappears. Then as you move it nearer the induced pole reappears and attracts the metal - this time it's a south pole. The polarity swap is shown by the compass experiment later in the vid. There being a definate polarity swap - it makes sense to me the that at some point the is a zero force

author: @rogerscottq
text: Are you sure the magnetism disappears? You have the rear pole holding the keeper and the paper clip is now undergoing induction according to the greater contact, which is opposed to the side separated. Bringing the keeper closer to the contrasting pole now induces an opposite current so that there should, in the paper-clip, be a repulsion. This 'cancelling' seems all fairly straight-forward. The equipotential zone in the keeper is moving at the same time. Or maybe I don't understand. Fascinating
